title: Lesson Quiz: Measuring the Economy (GDP)
description: Assessment of the concept that Gross Domestic Product and economic output.

questions.prompt: What does Gross Domestic Product (GDP) measure?

choices.text: The total value of final goods and services produced within a country over a specific period of time
choices.feedback: Correct. GDP measures total economic output produced within a country during a period of time.

choices.text: The number of people who are unemployed in a country
The average price of all goods in the economy
choices.feedback: That choice refers more to prices and inflation than to GDP.

choices.text: The total amount of taxes collected by the government
choices.feedback: Taxes are not the same as GDP. GDP measures production.

questions.prompt: Consumption is one of the main components of GDP.

choices.feedback: Correct. Consumption is one of the four major components of GDP.
This is incorrect. Consumption is a major part of GDP.

questions.prompt: Which activity is the best example of investment in GDP?

choices.text: A family buying groceries for the week
choices.feedback: Buying groceries is consumption, not investment.

choices.text: A business buying new machines for production
choices.feedback: Correct. Business spending on equipment is counted as investment in GDP.

choices.text: A government paying for road repairs
choices.feedback: That is government spending, not investment in this GDP category.

choices.text: A student buying a movie ticket
choices.feedback: A movie ticket is part of consumption, not investment.

questions.prompt: What are net exports in GDP?

choices.feedback: Net exports are not found by adding exports and imports.
Correct. Net exports are calculated as exports minus imports.
This does not describe net exports.
This is not how net exports are measured.

questions.prompt: Economists use GDP to compare economic output over time and across countries.

choices.feedback: Correct. GDP helps economists compare the size and performance of economies.
This is incorrect. GDP is commonly used to compare economic output over time and across countries.
